title: The Nicene Creed, Part 5
speaker: Mr. Alex Lyro
source_files: The Nicene Creed __ Part 5 (Mr. Alex Lyro).txt
outline: Part 5 of the Nicene Creed series
Exposition of the relevant article(s) of the Nicene Creed
summary: Part 5 of the October Sunday School series on the Nicene Creed, presented by Mr. Alex Lyro. Continues the verse-by-verse exposition of the Creed in its historical and theological context.
transcript:
## Series Review

In 325, Constantine called a council of bishops to determine whether Arius's theology should be commended or condemned. The very personhood of Christ was being called into question, but from that controversy came a creed that beautifully captures what it is that the Christian believes. Of course, the Creed of Nicaea is not what we have today. It was not until 381 that the Nicene Creed we have today would be completed — almost. We will get to that.

We started off with "We believe." Elder Nick Anderson showed that Christianity isn't built on feelings or opinions, but on revealed truth — a shared conviction rooted in God's self-revelation. Elder Scott Hollander addressed "the only begotten" and "one substance." We were reminded that Jesus is in fact God of God, light of light, very God of very God — simply put, God the Father and God the Son are of the same substance. This morning we heard from Mr. Isaiah Couch. We learned about Christ's humiliation and exaltation. He conveyed to us the wonder of redemption — the God who is eternal, almighty, and Triune has acted in love to save sinners.

And today we come to the Holy Spirit: "the Lord and Giver of life, who proceeds from the Father and the Son."

## The *Filioque*: Historical Background

To unpack this phrase we have to start with some history. I know it's Sunday afternoon, but this history is of the very essence of where this came from.

The Nicene Creed we have today is the same as the creed produced by the Council of Constantinople in 381 — almost. That creed did not include three words: "and the Son." Their original statement read: "The Holy Spirit, the Lord and Giver of life, who proceeds from the Father." It was not until the end of the sixth century that those words were added.

In 589, the *filioque* was added. *Filio* is Latin for "Son" and *-que* is a suffix meaning "and." After Rome had fallen in the mid-400s, the Gothic tribes were converting to Christianity in Spain. However, the Christianity they were converting to was Arianism. A council was called in Toledo by Leander, the Bishop of Seville, who was working hard to convert the Gothic tribes to true Christianity — aided by Reccared, the Visigoth king who had himself been converted. Most of the Arians in the area didn't think there was any point in attending the council, so they didn't. Due to the lack of Arian presence, the Nicene Creed was easily affirmed, and in an attempt to protect the deity of the Spirit, the *filioque* was added — though it was likely drawn from an existing translation of the Nicene Creed already circulating among Christians.

This caused far more division than anyone planned, and eventually contributed to what is commonly known as the Great Schism of 1054. If you want to know more about the papacy and the Great Schism, Elder Nick Anderson did a wonderful Wednesday night series on it — I don't remember if it was five years ago or ten years ago, but that's what it covered.

## Does the *Filioque* Belong? Is It True?

The clause "who proceeds from the Father and the Son" is not mere tradition but a faithful summary of Scripture's teaching on the Holy Spirit's eternal procession within the Trinity. *Proceeds* describes the Spirit's timeless origin from the Father and the Son. Just as the Son is eternally begotten by the Father, so the Spirit eternally proceeds from the Father and the Son, affirming his full deity and the Godhead's unity — three persons, one essence.

This is grounded in Scripture. Jesus says in John 15:26:

> *"When the Helper comes, whom I shall send to you from the Father, the Spirit of truth who proceeds from the Father, he will testify of me."* (John 15:26)

Here the Spirit's procession from the Father is clear, but Jesus' role in sending him implies his involvement. A chapter later, in John 16:7, Jesus says:

> *"Nevertheless I tell you the truth. It is to your advantage that I go away; for if I do not go away, the Helper will not come to you; but if I depart, I will send him to you."* (John 16:7)

The Son's sending of the Spirit reflects their eternal relationship. And in Galatians 4, Paul says:

> *"And because you are sons, God has sent forth the Spirit of his Son into your hearts, crying out, 'Abba, Father!'"* (Galatians 4:6)

This clearly ties the mission of the Holy Spirit to both Father and Son. And in Romans 8:9:

> *"But you are not in the flesh but in the Spirit, if indeed the Spirit of God dwells in you. Now if anyone does not have the Spirit of Christ, he is not his."* (Romans 8:9)

Paul here equates the Spirit of God and the Spirit of Christ — not two different spirits or spirits of different degree, but the one Holy Spirit of the Godhead.

Augustine, in his writing on the Trinity, wrote: "The Holy Spirit, according to the Holy Scriptures, is neither of the Father alone nor of the Son alone, but of both" — and so he is the expression of a mutual love whereby the Father and the Son reciprocally love one another. In other words, the Spirit is a living bond of love between the Father and the Son. John Calvin echoes Augustine in his *Institutes*: "The Spirit proceeds from the Father and the Son as the bond of their mutual love." Calvin grounds this in John 20:22, where Jesus, as he commissions the disciples, breathes the Spirit onto them, reflecting Genesis 2:7, where God breathes life into man. The Holy Spirit is the Lord, the eternal Giver of life — at creation, at the disciples' commissioning, and today, as those who are dead are made alive by the Spirit.

Sinclair Ferguson puts it this way: "The Spirit is the Spirit of the Father and the Son, proceeding from both in eternal communion of the Trinity, and he unites us to Christ in an unbreakable bond of salvation." That same perfect eternal communion shared within the Godhead is shared with us, the believers. There is nothing that can separate us from the love of God.

## Practical Applications

So how does this theological truth translate to faith in practice? As we have heard in our series on 2 John, there is no doctrine that is not applicable to the race we run.

### 1. Worship in Awe

The Spirit, as the bond of divine love, makes worship alive. When you come into God's house of worship, worship him as the Triune God. That should not be an afterthought for us. But there is a disconnect between the fact that the Holy Spirit is in fact God and how we actually live.

In this American culture we are cursed with the convenience that breeds an attitude of indifference. I don't know if anyone here is familiar with John Guerra, but he is a Christian artist who makes beautiful music. In his most recent album, he talks about the teaching of Jesus and says that we, as Christians in the modern world, don't doubt that Jesus can turn stone into bread — it's just that we're not really that hungry.

None of us would deny the deity of the Holy Spirit, yet we act as if he is a mystical force that comes and goes. In practice we don't act as if he is God, though we would never say he is not God. We do not have to wait for the Spirit to move to worship truly — the Spirit of God is within you. Jesus says in John 16 that the Spirit will glorify him. How? In many ways, but largely through the gathering of believers on the Lord's Day, worshiping him.

People like to say, after a good sermon or a good song when the lights are down low, "Man, I really felt the Spirit moving today." I will say — if you're in the front rows, it is a little easier to feel the Spirit moving than if you're way in the back. But there are no days when the Spirit does not move. Approach the Lord's table with reverence, sing hymns with wonder, and listen to the word preached as hearers and doers.

The Holy Spirit does dwell in you, as Paul tells us in 1 Corinthians 3:16. Understand the responsibility you have been given — as bearers of God's image, as recipients of Christ's atonement, and as temples of the Holy Spirit.

### 2. Unity

This truth should also lead us to unity. The same Spirit who is united with the Father and the Son secures our salvation and unites the church.

> *"I do not pray for these alone, but also for those who will believe in me through their word; that they all may be one, as you, Father, are in me, and I in you; that they also may be one in us, that the world may believe that you sent me. And the glory which you gave me I have given them, that they may be one just as we are one."* (John 17:20–22)

Jesus, as he awaits his agonizing death, prays for you and me. He prays that we may dwell in unity, having love for one another. How often we choose to make that prayer empty — letting pride reign in our relationships, grieving the Spirit that he has breathed into us, and deciding that the gods we make of ourselves are better than the God we have been brought into. Romans 12 urges us to live peaceably with all men.

Husbands, when you lead your family, do so through the Spirit — the same Spirit who glorifies the Son and serves the Father with joy. A husband filled with the Spirit leads not by pride or force, but by sacrificial love as prophet, priest, and king. He prays with his wife, listens to her, and bears her burdens as Christ bore ours. The union of the Godhead shows us that submission is not inferiority, but harmony born of divine love. The Son delights to glorify the Father, and the Spirit delights to glorify the Son. Spirit-filled submission reflects a heavenly truth. A wife who walks in the Spirit mirrors the beauty of that eternal fellowship, bringing gentleness, wisdom, and grace that adorns the gospel. And children, you too share in this fellowship — when you obey your parents, you give the world a glimpse of God.

How are we united as a church? Ephesians 4:1–3 says:

> *"I, therefore, the prisoner of the Lord, beseech you to walk worthy of the calling with which you were called, with all lowliness and gentleness, with longsuffering, bearing with one another in love, endeavoring to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace."* (Ephesians 4:1–3)

A church united in the name of God is a powerful thing, but it is so easily cut down from within when members do not choose to reconcile — even though, as brothers and sisters in Christ, we are united by the Spirit. Remember, we are one body, Christ's body, which should strive to be a reflection of our Triune God's divine love. Be humble. Serve joyfully. Forget yourself.

Now it is worth pointing out that the unity of the Spirit does not mean uniformity. The Father is not the Son; the Son is not the Spirit — yet they are perfectly one. The church should act likewise. Paul addresses this in 1 Corinthians 12:4:

> *"There are diversities of gifts, but the same Spirit. There are differences of ministries, but the same Lord. And there are diversities of activities, but it is the same God who works all in all. But the manifestation of the Spirit is given to each one for the profit of all."* (1 Corinthians 12:4–7)

When we use our gifts for one another's good, we reflect the life of the Triune God — distinct persons working in perfect cooperation. But wherever the Spirit brings unity, the flesh will try its best to bring division. Pride, gossip, jealousy, doctrinal indifference — all these grieve the Spirit who proceeds from the Father and the Son. Paul warns us again in Galatians 5:15:

> *"But if you bite and devour one another, beware lest you be consumed by one another! I say then: walk in the Spirit, and you shall not fulfill the lust of the flesh."* (Galatians 5:15–16)

Theological precision is important, but precision without love divides, and love without truth deceives. The Spirit of the Father and the Son produces both truth with tenderness and conviction with compassion. When disagreements in the church arise — and they have and they will — ask yourself: am I walking in the Spirit or in the flesh? Because where the Spirit reigns, peace reigns.

Ultimately, however, unity is not for its own sake. The unity of the church is not an end in itself, but a witness to the world of who God is. When the world sees a group of redeemed sinners acting contrary to the flesh and loving one another across their differences, the world sees God — they catch a glimpse of the very fellowship of the Trinity.

> *"By this all will know that you are my disciples, if you have love for one another."* (John 13:35)

Let the church of Christ be a living picture of the Triune God: many members, one body; many voices, one confession; many sinners, one Savior.

### 3. Assurance of Salvation

The unity of the Godhead also gives us assurance of salvation. The phrase "who proceeds from the Father and the Son" tells us not only about the nature of God but also about the nature of our salvation. The same Spirit who eternally proceeds from both the Father and the Son is the one who comes to dwell in believers, bringing to us the very life and love of God.

> *"For you did not receive the spirit of bondage again to fear, but you received the Spirit of adoption by whom we cry out, 'Abba, Father.' The Spirit himself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God."* (Romans 8:15–16)

The word *proceeds* is not an abstract theological footnote — it is personal. The Spirit who proceeds from the Father and the Son now proceeds into your heart, making the reality of God's love not just a doctrine but a daily experience.

When we confess that the Spirit proceeds from the Father and the Son, we are confessing that he is fully God — not a created power or a mystical influence. Because he is fully God, his indwelling presence in us is the unshakable seal of our redemption.

> *"In him you also trusted, after you heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation; in whom also, having believed, you were sealed with the Holy Spirit of promise, who is the guarantee of our inheritance until the redemption of the purchased possession, to the praise of his glory."* (Ephesians 1:13–14)

The Spirit marks us as belonging to Christ. That mark does not come and go with our emotions. It does not fade as we doubt our worthiness. We are stamped with indelible ink as belonging to Christ. When doubts arise, remember that the same Spirit who hovered over the waters of creation now hovers over your soul, keeping faith alive until the day of redemption.

But you may ask: does the Spirit actually dwell in me? Does he hover over my soul? Ask yourself these things: Do you love Jesus? Paul says in 1 Corinthians that no one can say "Jesus is Lord" except by the Holy Spirit. Do you hunger for the word of God? Do you pray? Do you grieve over sin? Do you desire holiness? Paul says in Galatians: walk by the Spirit and you will not gratify the desires of the flesh. Do you see fruit in your life? Does your tree bear good fruit? Do you love the brethren? Do you persevere through trials? It is the Spirit who gives life; the flesh is no help at all, as Jesus says in John 6.

Simply put: do you believe in Christ? Do you battle sin? Do you bear fruit? You don't have to worry about your worthiness or whether the Spirit comes and goes in your heart. If you say you love Jesus and your tree is bearing fruit, the Spirit is within you.

Remember, the goal of the Spirit is to bear witness to Christ — not to your works, your worthiness, or your consistency. Yes, you must examine the fruit growing in your life. But Christian identity is not a farmer's market. We are not saved by the fruit we display as Jesus walks by. The fruit *shows* that you have faith. It is not what saves you. The Spirit's work is not to make you gaze at yourself but to fix your eyes on Christ. Every time the Spirit strengthens your faith, warms your heart in worship, or brings conviction followed by repentance, it is as if he is whispering in your ear: *"See — you belong to the Father, because of the Son."*

## Conclusion

So brothers and sisters, when you recite the Creed and say "who proceeds from the Father and the Son," remember what that means. The Spirit who unites heaven itself has united you to Christ forever. He will never abandon you; he will never lose you. You are held in the fellowship of the Triune God — eternally loved, eternally kept, eternally home.

When guilt accuses you, when the devil whispers doubt, when your heart grows cold, remember that the Spirit who proceeds from the Father and the Son also proceeds into your heart, crying out, "Abba, Father!" — a voice from heaven itself reminding you that you belong to God.
